votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of extracting the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b, multiplying them, and comparing the result to res, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of extracting the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b and comparing their product to res, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly computes the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b, and checks if their product equals res, exactly matching the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification: it computes the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b using modulo 10, multiplies them, and checks if res equals that product. Python's abs(), %, and == operations match Coq's Z.abs, mod, and equality for integers.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification: it computes the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b using modulo 10, multiplies them, and checks if res equals that product. Python's abs() and % operator on integers behave the same as Coq's Z.abs and Z.modulo for this purpose.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification: it computes the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b using modulo 10, multiplies them, and checks if res equals that product. Python's abs() and % operator behave the same as Coq's Z.abs and mod for integers, producing identical concrete results.
Both compute abs(a)%10 and abs(b)%10 and check res equals their product; Python’s % with nonnegative operands matches Coq’s mod for 10, so results coincide for all inputs.
Both compute the absolute values’ unit digits via % 10 and check that res equals their product; Python ints and modulo with positive 10 match Coq Z semantics on all inputs.
